175000,     0, 40000, 90000, 150000, 300000   ),   policy_years = rep(1, 10) )  thresholds <- assess_excess_threshold(   portfolio,   claim_amount = "claim_amount",   thresholds = c(25000, 50000, 100000, 150000),   exposure = "policy_years",   group = "sector",   claim_count = "claim_count" )  if (requireNamespace("gt", quietly = TRUE)) {   as_gt(thresholds) } else {   thresholds } adjusted <- redistribute_excess_loss(   portfolio,   claim_amount = "claim_amount",   threshold = 100000,   claim_count = "claim_count",   risk_factor = "sector",   redistribution_method = "partial",   output = "redistributed_claim" ) severity_data <- adjusted[adjusted$claim_count > 0, ] severity_model <- glm(   claim_amount_adjusted_average ~ sector,   weights = claim_count,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = severity_data ) loading_result <- redistribute_excess_loss(   portfolio,   claim_amount = "claim_amount",   threshold = 100000,   claim_count = "claim_count",   redistribution_weight = "policy_years",   risk_factor = "sector",   redistribution_method = "partial",   output = "excess_loading" )  frequency_model <- glm(   claim_count ~ sector + offset(log(policy_years)),   family = poisson(link = "log"),   data = loading_result ) retained_severity_model <- glm(   claim_amount_capped ~ sector,   weights = claim_count,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = loading_result[loading_result$claim_count > 0, ] )  loading_result$predicted_frequency <-   predict(frequency_model, type = "response") / loading_result$policy_years loading_result$predicted_retained_severity <- predict(   retained_severity_model,   newdata = loading_result,   type = "response" ) loading_result$predicted_retained_risk_premium <-   loading_result$predicted_frequency *   loading_result$predicted_retained_severity loading_result$predicted_total_risk_premium <-   loading_result$predicted_retained_risk_premium +   loading_result$excess_loading

code: mod1 <- glm(nclaims ~ age_policyholder,             data = MTPL,             offset = log(exposure),             family = poisson())  # Add predicted claim frequency mtpl_pred <- add_prediction(MTPL, mod1, predictions = "pred_frequency")  # Add predicted values with confidence bounds mtpl_pred_ci <- add_prediction(   MTPL,   mod1,   predictions = "pred_frequency",   confidence = TRUE )  # Combine frequency and severity predictions into a pure premium proxy freq <- glm(nclaims ~ bm + zip,             data = MTPL,             offset = log(exposure),             family = poisson())  sev <- glm(amount ~ bm + zip,            data = MTPL[MTPL$amount > 0, ],            weights = nclaims,            family = Gamma(link = "log"))  premium_proxy <- add_prediction(   MTPL,   freq,   sev,   predictions = c("pred_frequency", "pred_severity") )  premium_proxy$pred_pure_premium <-   premium_proxy$pred_frequency * premium_proxy$pred_severity

text: model_variable variable already used GLM. split_variable detailed variable portfolio data used split one levels model_variable. relativities argument named list describing splits, usually built relativities() split_level(). step stored rating_refinement object applied refit() called. normalize = TRUE, supplied relativities normalised using exposure refined split keeps original level effect average. helps prevent expert split unintentionally changing total premium level original model group. use add_relativities() intended refinement within already reasonably homogeneous GLM segment. redistributes existing coefficient across sublevels using exposure-weighted relativities, preserving overall level original coefficient. useful mild heterogeneity, commercial refinement, monotonic tariff differentiation, expert-based segmentation within stable risk group original GLM coefficient broadly representative. Limitations method substitute creating separate risk segment original GLM coefficient distorted. example, suppose broad industry segment contains many relatively stable businesses, chemical companies drive losses representing little exposure. fitted industry coefficient may dominated chemical companies' experience. Applying exposure-weighted relativities inside segment may barely reduce coefficient large exposure group, original coefficient already pulled upward outlier subgroup. situation often better create separate GLM factor level, derive separate tariff segment, apply explicit segmentation acceptation rules, instead relying add_relativities().

title: Smooth grouped tariff relativities — add_smoothing
text: available methods represent different assumptions shape tariff effect: "spline" Fits unconstrained global polynomial grouped GLM relativities. degree determines order. low degree generally easier interpret less sensitive near boundaries; higher degrees can follow local variation may introduce oscillation. "gam" Fits unconstrained penalized smooth mgcv::gam(). allows tariff curve adapt observed pattern smoothing penalty limits unnecessary variation. "mpi" "mpd" Fit monotone increasing monotone decreasing P-splines, respectively. "cx" "cv" Fit convex concave P-splines, respectively. "micx" "micv" Fit monotone increasing curves , respectively, convex concave. "mdcx" "mdcv" Fit monotone decreasing curves , respectively, convex concave. shape-constrained methods fitted scam::scam(). constraint reflect actuarial pricing assumption defensible risk factor; selected solely produces smoother visual result.

title: Smooth grouped tariff relativities — add_smoothing
text: "gam" shape-constrained methods, k specifies basis dimension. controls maximum flexibility available smooth, final effective degrees freedom fitted curve. penalized GAM, estimated smoothing penalty can reduce effective degrees freedom maximum. smaller k restricts curve broad movements. larger k permits local variation, requires enough distinct grouped covariate values may unstable tariff levels available. k NULL, effective default basis dimension 10 used. function checks dimension grouped values fitting reports observed number unique values requested complexity feasible. "spline", degree corresponding complexity role. polynomial degree \(d\) requires least \(d + 1\) unique grouped values. degree omitted, existing behaviour uses highest degree supported grouped model points. deprecated smooth_coef() wrapper remains available backwards compatibility.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(dplyr)  age_policyholder_frequency <- risk_factor_gam(   data = MTPL,   claim_count = "nclaims",   risk_factor = "age_policyholder",   exposure = "exposure" )  age_segments_freq <- derive_tariff_segments(age_policyholder_frequency)  dat <- MTPL |>   add_tariff_segments(age_segments_freq, name = "age_policyholder_freq_cat") |>   mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) |>   mutate(across(where(is.factor), ~ set_reference_level(., exposure)))  freq <- glm(   nclaims ~ bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   offset = log(exposure),   family = poisson(),   data = dat )  sev <- glm(   amount ~ zip,   weights = nclaims,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = dat |> filter(amount > 0) )  premium_df <- dat |>   add_prediction(freq, sev) |>   mutate(premium = pred_nclaims_freq * pred_amount_sev)  burn_unrestricted <- glm(   premium ~ zip + bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   weights = exposure,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = premium_df )  ref <- prepare_refinement(burn_unrestricted) |>   add_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_policyholder_freq_cat",     source_variable = "age_policyholder",     breaks = seq(18, 95, 5),     smoothing = "gam",     k = 6,     weights = "exposure"   )  # Limit the visible range without changing the fitted smoothing curve. autoplot(ref, x_max = 80, y_max = 1.5) } # }

title: Edit an existing smoothing step in a refinement workflow — edit_smoothing
text: Martin Haringa

title: Edit an existing smoothing step in a refinement workflow — edit_smoothing
code: set.seed(42) driver_age <- rep(seq(20, 59), each = 4) exposure <- rep(1, length(driver_age)) age_band <- cut(   driver_age,   breaks = c(18, 30, 40, 50, 60),   include.lowest = TRUE ) expected_claims <- exp(   -1.7 + 0.018 * (driver_age - 20) + 0.0006 * (driver_age - 40)^2 ) portfolio <- data.frame(   claims = rpois(length(driver_age), exposure * expected_claims),   exposure = exposure,   driver_age = driver_age,   age_band = age_band )  model <- glm(   claims ~ age_band + offset(log(exposure)),   family = poisson(),   data = portfolio )  refined <- prepare_refinement(model, data = portfolio) |>   add_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_band",     source_variable = "driver_age",     breaks = c(18, 30, 40, 50, 60),     degree = 2,     weights = "exposure"   ) |>   edit_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_band",     from = 30,     to = 50,     from_value = 1.00,     to_value = 1.10,     control_positions = c(40),     control_values = c(1.05)   )  refined_model <- refit(refined)

title: Fit severity distributions to truncated claim data — fit_truncated_severity
text: insurance pricing, severity models often fitted claim amounts observed full range possible losses. Small claims may absent deductible, reporting threshold, data extraction rule. large claims may capped, excluded, modelled separately large losses. standard gamma lognormal fit remaining observed claims treats truncated sample complete, can bias estimated severity distribution. fit_truncated_severity() fits distribution conditional claim observed within truncation interval. means fitted likelihood uses density divided probability mass lower_truncation upper_truncation. function intended truncation, claims outside interval absent data. differs censoring, claims outside limit still observed exact amount known. Observed losses must lie strictly inside truncation interval. Values outside interval indicate bounds describe data therefore produce error.

title: Fit a GAM for a continuous risk factor — risk_factor_gam
text: data data.frame containing insurance portfolio. risk_factor Character, name column data continuous risk factor. claim_count Character, name column data number claims. exposure Character, name column data exposure. claim_amount (Optional) Character, column name data claim amount. Required model = "severity". pure_premium (Optional) Character, column name data pure premium. Required model = "pure_premium". model Character string specifying model type. One "frequency", "severity", "pure_premium". Default "frequency". old value "burning" deprecated maps "pure_premium". round_risk_factor (Optional) Numeric value round risk factor multiple round_risk_factor. Can speed fitting factors many distinct values. x, nclaims, amount, round_x Deprecated argument names. Use risk_factor, claim_count, claim_amount, round_risk_factor instead.

title: Fit a GAM for a continuous risk factor — risk_factor_gam
text: list class "riskfactor_gam" following elements: prediction data frame predicted values confidence intervals. x Name continuous risk factor. model model type: "frequency", "severity", "pure_premium". data Merged data frame predictions observed values. x_obs Observed values continuous risk factor.

title: Fit a GAM for a continuous risk factor — risk_factor_gam
text: Frequency model: Fits Poisson GAM number claims. log exposure used offset expected number claims proportional exposure. Severity model: Fits Gamma GAM log link average claim size (total amount divided number claims). number claims included weight. Pure premium model: Fits Gamma GAM log link pure premium (risk premium). Implemented aggregating exposure-weighted pure premiums. deprecated model value "burning" still accepted backward compatibility.

title: Fit a GAM for a continuous risk factor — risk_factor_gam
text: function fit_gam() deprecated version 0.8.0 replaced risk_factor_gam(). addition name change, interface also changed: fit_gam() used non-standard evaluation (NSE), column names passed unquoted (e.g. x = age_policyholder). risk_factor_gam() uses standard evaluation (SE), column names must passed character strings (e.g. risk_factor = "age_policyholder"). makes function easier use programmatic workflows. riskfactor_gam() fit_gam() still available backward compatibility emit deprecation warnings.

title: Summarise redistributed large-loss experience — summary.excess_redistribution
text: redistributed_excess_contributed excess amount removed selected large losses segment. redistributed_excess_received amount assigned back claim-bearing rows segment. difference : $$ net\_loss\_shift = received - contributed $$ positive value means segment receives redistributed loss contributed. negative value means transfers loss segments. Across full portfolio, net_loss_shift sums zero, subject numerical tolerance. = NULL, summary uses risk_factor supplied redistribute_excess_loss(). risk factor supplied, one portfolio row returned. Supply inspect portfolio redistribution another portfolio characteristic, example summary(x, = "sector"). summary also exposes loading calculation. partial redistribution, <risk_factor>_excess_loading blended portfolio_excess_loading using <risk_factor>_credibility. resulting blended_excess_loading multiplied redistribution_scaling_factor obtain final_redistribution_loading. Multiplying final loading total receiving redistribution_weight gives redistributed_excess_received. differs risk factor used redistribution, loading credibility columns receiving-weighted averages within audit group. separate-loading output, adjusted_loss shown reconciliation retained plus allocated loss; mean allocated amount added row-level severity response.
